speaker: interviewer
text: I'm rolling right now, I'm rolling right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five, interviewing Malka Baran. Uh, can you tell me, uh, whom we have on this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the earliest picture that I got here in United States, when I found my friend and she had that picture. I don't have anything from home myself, because we were just taken out too quickly. We didn't take any pictures with us. On this picture, there are eight girls, friends from my neighborhood. The best friend was the second one in the second row. And she survived. Next to her I am, in the second row, and I survived. All the other six were murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your two friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one that is next to me is Hella [POLISH]. And she's now Hella Zilberberg. She's still here in United States. She had this picture and we made a copy for me. And was very exciting. This was done in the beginning of the war, in the large ghetto, before the [NON-ENGLISH], before the selection. And you can see one of us is showing the star. We all wore them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to clear. Where are you again? On--

speaker: interviewee
text: The third one from the top, from left. In the top row.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was probably '40 or '39. When the-- in '40 probably, when the ghetto started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who 's the girl wearing the, uh, Star of David band?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther [POLISH]. That was also one of my best friends. And the other names unfortunately I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, How old are they, young?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are all approximately 15. This page is from a memory book from-- Helen gave it to me, my friend who gave me also the other picture. She had it in her-- um, in memory, not in memor-- but from friends, a book that children in school sign at the end of the year. And my brother made-- signed it for her. And it says-- uh,

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read it in Polish first?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] H. Klin, Heniek Klin. [SPEAKING POLISH]. Um, 2nd of--

speaker: interviewer
text: August.

speaker: interviewee
text: August, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stan wojenny, it says. That's 'during the war.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you translate it for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, 'To the big child,' he called her a big child, because she was very tall and a little bit heavy. Uh, 'In memory, to remember-- to remember me, Heniek Klin.' Czestochowa is the name of our town, city, and the date. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: February-- no, uh, August 2, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is--

speaker: interviewee
text: And 'stan wojenny' means during the war. Wartime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wartime.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wartime.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, what-- whom do we have on this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture I just got recently. Last summer, when I was in Israel, I found a relative and asked that maybe you have some pictures. And she looked through the pile of pictures, and she didn't know who they were. And I immediately got excited. This is my dear, dear, aunt, my father's brother's wife, with five children-- four sons and one girl-- who all-- all perished. All were killed in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is she seated?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's seated probably somewhere in a park, I don't know exactly where it was. But, uh, those are her sons. One son survived. He left Poland very early on, and he was in Israel when I arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these are all--

speaker: interviewee
text: All my first cousins. And all were killed. With [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, can you tell us who is on this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's, uh, our wedding picture in Leah's house, the Leah who adopted me, my dear, dear friend and family. I still call her my mom. She's only two years older than I am. Uh, her home became my home. And she insisted to make our wedding in her house. And she baked and cooked for everything. In those days, we didn't have halls. And I'm not-- I'm not sure I would like to be married in a very formal hall-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: --ceremony. That was in '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: December 25, '52 was the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Leah's house in Ramat Gan, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm rolling right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, can you tell me, uh, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's a very interesting story. That's my Aunt Luba, the one whom I mentioned when I talked about my girlfriends in school wanting her picture as an actress. I was sure that she did not survive the war. She lived in Warsaw with her entire family, with a husband and child. Uh, in 1960, that is 15 years after the end of the war, she found me. She sent a letter from Isr-- from Poland or Israel-- from Israel-- that starting, 'This is your Aunt Luba.' And I almost fainted. I was in the hospital at that time, after the surgery. And the way she survived was she ran away from Warsaw Ghetto before the ghetto was closed, just the day before, with the baby, and traveled on foot and by, uh, wagons and whoever was kind enough to take her, to Russia. And she survived with the child in Russia. Of course, then after the war she searched everywhere, and she asked everywhere she was whether they know a fam-- family from Czestochowa by this name. And finally she found someone who knew, who knew someone who knew me. And that's how she found me, 15 years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this-- where this picture is taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. She gave it to me when I met with her for the first time. That was in '60-- '61? Or '60. I went to Israel to meet with her, with my two girls. Uh, Bella was six and Avi was four. And I got the pic--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself, graduating from Queens College, very proud of my achievement and smiling, as you see. Wearing the cape and gown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year--

speaker: interviewee
text: Cap and gown.

speaker: interviewer
text: --roughly, it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh, either '72 or '75, my first, uh, graduation or the master's degree. I think it was my first, '72.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's our younger daughter who lives in Pittsburgh, Avi, and Paul at their wedding in Providence, Rhode Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's our older daughter, Bella, and Amos in Israel in Jerusalem, married in '87.

speaker: interviewer
text: The wedding was in Jerusalem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. And we were all there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll ask you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the pic-- children on this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are our granddaughters in Israel. Maya, the older one, and Avia, the younger one. They are about six and four in that picture. And they are in their home balcony in Jerusalem, Gilo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, who is the shorter girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avia.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's the younger one?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's the younger one, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Um, rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, uh, what is the importance of this, uh, heart, uh, for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course. This is my most precious possession. I always wear it. This was given-- and only thing from home, really. It was given to me for my last birthday, January 30, '42. I was 15. And it has my monogram, MK, one side. And on the other side-- can we turn it? It says--

speaker: interviewer
text: He stopped rolling. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: On the other side, it says in Polish [SPEAKING POLISH]. 'From'-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which means?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my par-- 'From your parents.' And the date it was given to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was 30th-- Nov-- uh, January 30, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, is the chain the original chain?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the chain, as I mentioned before in my story, was sold to [POLISH], for one bread. But after the war, of course, I put in on this chain. And the heart was saved in my skirt's, uh, waistband. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, can you introduce us to the gentleman sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my dear husband, Moshe Baran. And we were married in Austria-- in, uh, Israel. And he will tell you his story soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: (WHISPERING) But you wanted to say [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there-- is there something you would like to say to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. I want to thank you for 44 wonderful years. I want to thank you for his gentleness and love and sense of humor and understanding. And he really made me what I am, a very happy human being, and a happy wife and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to say to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to say that I love her wouldn't-- would be an understatement. And I'm very proud of her achievements. And beside being, uh, my wife, she's also my best friend. We, uh, I think we complement each other. She is, uh, more interested in the arts, and, uh, I'm-- I'm more interested in daily life and politics. And, uh, she-- the main thing is that she's challenging me. And this is what makes it more interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, Ms. Baran, is there anything else, uh, you would like to say before we come to an end of your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, you're talking? Uh, I want to say that I was blessed with a lot of wonderful gifts. And especially with our two daughters, who are both very gifted and bright and wonderful human beings, and developed quite wonderfully, despite the fact that we are Holocaust survivor. I don't know why I say despite the fact, because many families are like that. But, uh, we are very grateful for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And our grandchildren, of course, which we-- whom we showed, and for many, many blessings.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said it about my husband before.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, but-- no, that's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I want to thank you for giving your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. Why are you whispering while I'm talking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you missed the husbands--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh, you wanted to say something additional?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to dedicate this tape especially to my children, grandchildren, and my husband of course, and my sons-in-law. But I-- I'm thinking particularly about all the people that I met along the way. Uh, when I was liberated from camp, I was sort of numb and had to learn to live again. And every step toward life I made was aided by a certain person whom I met along the way, a humane, wonderful human being. And to all of those people, I want to dedicate this film, with great-- grateful thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
